__doc: Labeled agreement set for the eval judge swap (issue #1447). Pairs = every (golden, candidate-finding) the recall assertion judges. Reference labels added by run-agreement.js.

pairs.golden: Case sensitivity bypass in email blacklist
pairs.candidate: Team admins are incorrectly blocked because the permission check requires admin and owner simultaneously. — WHAT: `isTeamAdminOrOwner` requires the caller to be both a team admin and a team owner. WHY: team admins who are not owners will be rejected by the `FORBIDDEN` branch and lose access to the new add-guests action on team bookings. HOW: use `||` between `isTeamAdmin` and `isTeamOwner` so either role is sufficient. — bug — packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/bookings/addGuests.handler.ts

pairs.golden: Case sensitivity bypass in email blacklist
pairs.candidate: Notification recipients can diverge from the guests actually added to the booking. — WHAT: the handler emails the original `guests` input instead of the filtered `uniqueGuests` that were actually inserted. WHY: duplicate, already-attending, or blacklisted addresses can receive add-guest notifications even though they were not added to the booking or synced to the calendar. HOW: pass `uniqueGuests` to `sendAddGuestsEmails` so notifications match the persisted attendee set. — bug — packages/trpc/server/routers/viewer/bookings/addGuests.handler.ts

pairs.golden: Case sensitivity bypass in email blacklist
pairs.candidate: The permission check requires a user to be both team admin and team owner, incorrectly rejecting users who are only one of the two allowed roles. — The authorization logic for the new `addGuests` flow is stricter than the stated `team admin or owner` behavior. The analysis says `isTeamAdminOrOwner` is computed with `isTeamAdmin(...) && isTeamOwner(...)`, which means users who are only a team admin or only a team owner will fail the later permission gate. This is a concrete regression because the check should allow either role, not require both. — Incorrect admin/owner authorization logic — bookings/_router.tsx

pairs.golden: Case sensitivity bypass in email blacklist
pairs.candidate: The mailer is invoked with the unfiltered guest list instead of the deduplicated/validated guest list that was saved. — The email notification side effect operates on a different attendee set than the one that is actually persisted. According to the analysis, the handler stores only `uniqueGuests` after filtering existing attendees and blacklisted entries, but it passes the original `guests` array to `sendAddGuestsEmails`. Since `sendAddGuestsEmails` sends notifications for exactly the `newGuests: string[]` it receives, this can notify addresses that were never inserted. — Emails sent to guests not actually persisted — packages/emails/email-manager.ts

pairs.golden: The translation is in Italian instead of Lithuanian. This should be translated to Lithuanian to match the file's locale (messages_lt.properties).
pairs.candidate: The new verifier crashes on translated bundles whose English reference file does not exist at the synthesized `_en.properties` path. — WHAT: verifySafeHtml unconditionally rewrites every translated bundle path to a sibling `resources/..._en.properties` file and throws a RuntimeException when that file is absent. WHY: this turns verification of community translation bundles such as `themes/src/main/resources-community/theme/base/email/messages/messages_lt.properties` into a hard build failure instead of emitting validation messages, because the corresponding English files are not stored as `_en.properties` siblings in the checked tree. HOW: resolve the real source bundle location before opening it, or treat a missing English bundle as an empty/reference-less bundle instead of throwing. — bug — misc/theme-verifier/src/main/java/org/keycloak/themeverifier/VerifyMessageProperties.java

pairs.golden: The translation is in Italian instead of Lithuanian. This should be translated to Lithuanian to match the file's locale (messages_lt.properties).
pairs.candidate: A missing English source key now causes translation-only HTML entries to fail validation unconditionally. — WHAT: missing keys in the English bundle are converted to an empty string and then forced through `POLICY_NO_HTML`, so any translation-only `*Html` key is always reported as illegal HTML. WHY: this breaks the verifier's own fallback contract in `getEnglishValue`—the code explicitly tolerates a missing source key, but the next branch still rejects valid translated HTML because `containsHtml("")` is false. HOW: skip HTML-policy validation when the English key is missing, or infer allowance from the translated key name/value instead of treating the missing source as plain text. — bug — misc/theme-verifier/src/main/java/org/keycloak/themeverifier/VerifyMessageProperties.java

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: CountDevices and the subsequent write are not atomic, so the configured device limit can be exceeded under concurrency. — WHAT: the device-limit check is a non-atomic count-then-insert sequence, so two concurrent CreateOrUpdateDevice calls can both pass `count < s.deviceLimit` and both insert/update devices. WHY: this breaks the configured limit under concurrent anonymous traffic because the unique index only deduplicates by device_id, not by total active-device count. HOW: enforce the limit inside a single transaction with locking, or move the limit decision into a database-side primitive that serializes writers. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/anonstore/database.go

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: Existing devices older than 30 days but not yet cleaned up cannot be refreshed once the limit is full, causing rejected anonymous logins. — WHAT: when the limit is reached, updateDevice refuses to update any existing row whose `updated_at` is more than 30 days old, even though the row is still retained for 61 days. WHY: a returning anonymous device that last visited 31-61 days ago will hit `ErrDeviceLimitReached`, and client.go now turns that into an authentication failure instead of refreshing the existing device. HOW: match updates by `device_id` alone when reusing an existing device, or use the same retention window as cleanup if staleness must be checked. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/anonstore/database.go

pairs.golden: Anonymous authentication now fails entirely if anonDeviceService.TagDevice returns ErrDeviceLimitReached. Previously, device tagging was asynchronous and non-blocking. This change prevents anonymous users from authenticating when the device limit is reached.

pairs.golden: SSRF vulnerability using open(url) without validation
pairs.candidate: Feed polling crashes on entries without a `content` field. — WHAT: poll_feed unconditionally calls `i.content.scrub` for every feed item even though RSS/Atom entries can omit a content element. WHY: raises a NoMethodError on feeds that provide only summary/description fields, aborting the polling job and preventing all later items from being imported. HOW: guard for missing content and fall back to another body field before calling String methods, e.g. `body = i.content || i.description || i.summary || ''`. — bug — app/jobs/scheduled/poll_feed.rb

pairs.golden: The current origin validation using indexOf is insufficient and can be bypassed. An attacker could use a malicious domain like evil-discourseUrl.com to pass this check.
pairs.candidate: Feed polling crashes on entries without a `content` field. — WHAT: poll_feed unconditionally calls `i.content.scrub` for every feed item even though RSS/Atom entries can omit a content element. WHY: raises a NoMethodError on feeds that provide only summary/description fields, aborting the polling job and preventing all later items from being imported. HOW: guard for missing content and fall back to another body field before calling String methods, e.g. `body = i.content || i.description || i.summary || ''`. — bug — app/jobs/scheduled/poll_feed.rb

pairs.golden: Shared mutable default in dataclass timestamp
pairs.candidate: AssignmentSource deserialization does not round-trip the queued datetime across task payload serialization. — WHAT: `AssignmentSource.from_dict` accepts the queued field as-is, so task payloads that deserialize datetimes to strings reconstruct an `AssignmentSource` with a string `queued` value instead of a `datetime`. WHY: the new cycle-breaking context is carried through `assignment_source.to_dict()` into `sync_assignee_outbound`, and JSON-backed Celery serializers commonly stringify datetimes, so this method can silently return a malformed object and break any code that relies on `queued` being a real timestamp. HOW: parse `input_dict["queued"]` back to a `datetime` before calling the dataclass constructor, or exclude `queued` from the serialized payload if it is not needed for sync decisions. — bug — src/sentry/integrations/services/assignment_source.py

pairs.golden: Wrong parameter in null check (grantType vs. rawTokenId)
pairs.candidate: Using the reserved "na" shortcut inside duplicate validation can make valid grant providers prevent server startup. — WHAT: the reserved unknown grant shortcut "na" is inserted into the same maps as real grant shortcuts and the duplicate check treats any real grant type using shortcut "na" as a fatal map-size mismatch. WHY: a custom or future OAuth2GrantTypeFactory that legitimately returns "na" will make postInit throw IllegalStateException at startup, so enabling this feature can break server boot. HOW: keep the unknown sentinel outside the duplicate-detection maps or validate duplicates before inserting the reserved "na" entry. — bug — services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/protocol/oidc/encode/DefaultTokenContextEncoderProviderFactory.java

pairs.golden: Wrong parameter in null check (grantType vs. rawTokenId)
pairs.candidate: Reserved unknown shortcut "na" can collide with a real grant registration and break token-id round-tripping. — WHAT: the factory reserves the literal grant shortcut/id "na" in the same maps used for real OAuth2GrantType registrations without checking whether a provider already uses that value. WHY: a grant type whose id or shortcut is "na" is silently overwritten by the fallback entry, so token IDs for that legitimate grant will later decode as the synthetic UNKNOWN grant or fail to round-trip correctly. HOW: validate that no provider uses the reserved unknown marker before inserting it, or keep the unknown fallback outside the provider shortcut namespace. — bug — services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/protocol/oidc/encode/DefaultTokenContextEncoderProviderFactory.java

pairs.golden: In isAccessTokenId, the substring for the grant shortcut and the equality check look inverted: the grant shortcut occupies indices 4–5 (substring(4,6)), and a match should return true (combined with UUID check), not false.

pairs.golden: The parseRefreshTokenResponse function incorrectly sets refresh_token to the hardcoded string 'refresh_token' when it's missing from the OAuth refresh token response. This invalidates the token, breaking subsequent token refreshes and causing authentication failures.
pairs.candidate: Salesforce refresh stores new tokens but immediately connects with the expired old token. — WHAT: the refresh flow updates the credential row with the newly returned Salesforce tokens but then creates the jsforce connection from the stale `credentialKey` values instead of the refreshed token payload. WHY: when the stored access token is already expired, `getClient` still returns a connection using that expired access token and old instance URL, so the first Salesforce API call after a refresh can fail even though refresh succeeded. HOW: build the `jsforce.Connection` from `accessTokenParsed.data` (and preserve the old refresh token if the provider does not return one) rather than from `credentialKey`. — bug — packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts

pairs.golden: The parseRefreshTokenResponse function incorrectly sets refresh_token to the hardcoded string 'refresh_token' when it's missing from the OAuth refresh token response. This invalidates the token, breaking subsequent token refreshes and causing authentication failures.
pairs.candidate: Office365 now accepts the shared sync payload but does not translate its expiry field into the credential format this adapter stores. — WHAT: the Office365 refresh path now parses responses through `parseRefreshTokenResponse`, which switches to the generic shared-credential schema when sync is enabled, but this method still assumes `tokenResponse.data.expires_in` exists and merges it directly into the credential key. WHY: the shared sync endpoint explicitly returns only access token and expiry date, so synced Office365 credentials can be saved without an `expiry_date`/`expires_in` field and later token validation will treat them as invalid or repeatedly refresh them. HOW: normalize the shared response into Office365's stored credential shape before updating Prisma, e.g. map the generic expiry field to the property that `O365AuthCredentials` readers expect. — bug — packages/app-store/office365calendar/lib/CalendarService.ts

pairs.golden: The parseRefreshTokenResponse function incorrectly sets refresh_token to the hardcoded string 'refresh_token' when it's missing from the OAuth refresh token response. This invalidates the token, breaking subsequent token refreshes and causing authentication failures.
pairs.candidate: Concurrent webhook deliveries can create duplicate credentials for the same user/app pair. — WHAT: the webhook implements credential sync as `findFirst` followed by `create`/`update` without any uniqueness guarantee on `(userId, appId)`. WHY: two concurrent webhook deliveries for the same user and app can both miss `findFirst` and both insert, leaving duplicate credentials; later reads will update an arbitrary row because this code uses `findFirst`, so credentials become nondeterministic. HOW: enforce a unique key for `(userId, appId)` and use `upsert`, or wrap the read/write in a transaction that serializes on that pair. — bug — apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: ErrDeviceLimitReached returned from Authenticate blocks anonymous users from accessing Grafana instead of just stopping device tracking — WHAT: When the device limit is reached, `ErrDeviceLimitReached` is propagated from `TagDevice` → `Authenticate`, causing `Authenticate` to return an error that rejects the anonymous user entirely. WHY: Anonymous authentication should still succeed when the device limit is reached — the limit should only stop tracking new devices, not block access. This means any instance with `device_limit` configured will deny anonymous access to new devices rather than just stopping to track them. HOW: Do not return the error from `Authenticate`; instead just log it (similar to other non-fatal tagging errors). — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/client.go

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: updateDevice returns ErrDeviceLimitReached when device_id is not found (0 rows updated), which is the wrong semantic — the device simply doesn't exist yet — WHAT: In `updateDevice`, `rowsAffected == 0` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached`, but 0 rows affected means the device_id was not found in the table (it's a new device), not that the limit was reached — the limit was already checked by the caller. WHY: A new anonymous device that has never been tracked before will get `ErrDeviceLimitReached` even though the real problem is simply that the device doesn't exist; this wrong error propagates to `Authenticate` and can cause misleading behavior. HOW: Return a distinct sentinel (e.g., `ErrDeviceNotFound`) or return `nil` (silently ignore) when a new-device update hits 0 rows, since the intent is to skip inserting new devices beyond the limit. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/anonstore/database.go

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: Local cache is populated before the DB write succeeds, so a failed CreateOrUpdateDevice (ErrDeviceLimitReached) leaves a stale cache entry that bypasses enforcement on all subsequent requests from the same device — WHAT: In `tagDeviceUI`, `localCache.SetDefault(key, struct{}{})` is called before `CreateOrUpdateDevice` succeeds; if `CreateOrUpdateDevice` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached`, the cache entry persists for 29 minutes. WHY: On the next request from the same device, the local cache returns a hit and `tagDeviceUI` returns `nil` early, so `TagDevice` returns `nil` and `Authenticate` succeeds — the device is now authenticated without being tracked, making device-limit enforcement inconsistent (first request rejected, all subsequent requests from the same device silently pass). HOW: Move `localCache.SetDefault` to after the store call succeeds, or evict the cache key if the store returns an error. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/impl.go

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: TOCTOU race between CountDevices and insert allows concurrent requests to exceed the configured device limit — WHAT: `CountDevices` and the subsequent `updateDevice`/insert in `CreateOrUpdateDevice` are two separate DB operations with no transaction wrapping, creating a TOCTOU race condition. WHY: Two concurrent requests for different new devices can both read `count < deviceLimit`, both proceed past the limit check, and both insert new records, allowing the device table to grow beyond the configured limit. HOW: Wrap both `CountDevices` and the insert/update in a single transaction with appropriate isolation, or rely solely on a database-enforced unique constraint and table-level count check. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/anonstore/database.go

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: ErrDeviceLimitReached propagated from TagDevice causes Authenticate to deny anonymous access entirely instead of just skipping device tracking — WHAT: When the device limit is reached, `ErrDeviceLimitReached` is returned from `TagDevice` and then propagated as a hard error from `Authenticate`, causing anonymous authentication to fail entirely for any new device beyond the limit. WHY: Anonymous users are denied all access (receive an auth error response) simply because device tracking is at capacity — the device limit is meant to cap telemetry/tracking, not to block authentication. HOW: Treat `ErrDeviceLimitReached` the same as other non-critical tagging errors: log a warning and continue, returning the anonymous identity normally. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/client.go

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: Local cache is set before DB write succeeds, causing a failed CreateOrUpdateDevice to suppress retries for 29 minutes — WHAT: `tagDeviceUI` calls `a.localCache.SetDefault(key, struct{}{})` before `a.anonStore.CreateOrUpdateDevice` succeeds, so a failed DB write permanently poisons the local cache entry. WHY: For the next 29 minutes, any subsequent request from the same device hits the cache and short-circuits without retrying the DB write, silently losing device tracking until cache expiry. HOW: Move `localCache.SetDefault` to after `CreateOrUpdateDevice` returns nil. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/impl.go

pairs.golden: Race condition: Multiple concurrent requests could pass the device count check simultaneously and create devices beyond the limit. Consider using a database transaction or lock.
pairs.candidate: updateDevice returns ErrDeviceLimitReached when a known device's updated_at is outside the BETWEEN window, misclassifying a stale-but-valid device as a limit violation — WHAT: `updateDevice` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached` when `rowsAffected == 0`, but 0 rows are also affected when the device exists in the DB but its stored `updated_at` falls outside the `BETWEEN (now-30d) AND (now+1min)` window (e.g., a device last seen more than 30 days ago). WHY: A new anonymous device that has never been tracked before will get `ErrDeviceLimitReached` even though the real problem is simply that the device doesn't exist; this wrong error propagates to `Authenticate` and can cause misleading behavior. HOW: Return a distinct sentinel (e.g., `ErrDeviceNotFound`) or return `nil` (silently ignore) when a new-device update hits 0 rows, since the intent is to skip inserting new devices beyond the limit. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/anonstore/database.go

pairs.golden: Returning ErrDeviceLimitReached when no rows were updated is misleading; the device might not exist.
pairs.candidate: Local cache is populated before the DB write succeeds, so a failed CreateOrUpdateDevice (ErrDeviceLimitReached) leaves a stale cache entry that bypasses enforcement on all subsequent requests from the same device — WHAT: In `tagDeviceUI`, `localCache.SetDefault(key, struct{}{})` is called before `CreateOrUpdateDevice` succeeds; if `CreateOrUpdateDevice` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached`, the cache entry persists for 29 minutes. WHY: On the next request from the same device, the local cache returns a hit and `tagDeviceUI` returns `nil` early, so `TagDevice` returns `nil` and `Authenticate` succeeds — the device is now authenticated without being tracked, making device-limit enforcement inconsistent (first request rejected, all subsequent requests from the same device silently pass). HOW: Move `localCache.SetDefault` to after the store call succeeds, or evict the cache key if the store returns an error. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/impl.go

pairs.golden: Returning ErrDeviceLimitReached when no rows were updated is misleading; the device might not exist.
pairs.candidate: TOCTOU race between CountDevices and insert allows concurrent requests to exceed the configured device limit — WHAT: `CountDevices` and the subsequent `updateDevice`/insert in `CreateOrUpdateDevice` are two separate DB operations with no transaction wrapping, creating a TOCTOU race condition. WHY: Two concurrent requests for different new devices can both read `count < deviceLimit`, both proceed past the limit check, and both insert new records, allowing the device table to grow beyond the configured limit. HOW: Wrap both `CountDevices` and the insert/update in a single transaction with appropriate isolation, or rely solely on a database-enforced unique constraint and table-level count check. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/anonstore/database.go

pairs.golden: Returning ErrDeviceLimitReached when no rows were updated is misleading; the device might not exist.
pairs.candidate: ErrDeviceLimitReached propagated from TagDevice causes Authenticate to deny anonymous access entirely instead of just skipping device tracking — WHAT: When the device limit is reached, `ErrDeviceLimitReached` is returned from `TagDevice` and then propagated as a hard error from `Authenticate`, causing anonymous authentication to fail entirely for any new device beyond the limit. WHY: Anonymous users are denied all access (receive an auth error response) simply because device tracking is at capacity — the device limit is meant to cap telemetry/tracking, not to block authentication. HOW: Treat `ErrDeviceLimitReached` the same as other non-critical tagging errors: log a warning and continue, returning the anonymous identity normally. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/client.go

pairs.golden: Returning ErrDeviceLimitReached when no rows were updated is misleading; the device might not exist.
pairs.candidate: Local cache is set before DB write succeeds, causing a failed CreateOrUpdateDevice to suppress retries for 29 minutes — WHAT: `tagDeviceUI` calls `a.localCache.SetDefault(key, struct{}{})` before `a.anonStore.CreateOrUpdateDevice` succeeds, so a failed DB write permanently poisons the local cache entry. WHY: For the next 29 minutes, any subsequent request from the same device hits the cache and short-circuits without retrying the DB write, silently losing device tracking until cache expiry. HOW: Move `localCache.SetDefault` to after `CreateOrUpdateDevice` returns nil. — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/impl.go

pairs.golden: Returning ErrDeviceLimitReached when no rows were updated is misleading; the device might not exist.
pairs.candidate: updateDevice returns ErrDeviceLimitReached when a known device's updated_at is outside the BETWEEN window, misclassifying a stale-but-valid device as a limit violation — WHAT: `updateDevice` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached` when `rowsAffected == 0`, but 0 rows are also affected when the device exists in the DB but its stored `updated_at` falls outside the `BETWEEN (now-30d) AND (now+1min)` window (e.g., a device last seen more than 30 days ago). WHY: A legitimate, pre-existing device whose last-seen timestamp is older than 30 days is incorrectly classified as a device-limit violation, causing its anonymous authentication request to be hard-failed by the caller in `client.go`. HOW: The UPDATE WHERE clause should only filter by `device_id`, not additionally by the `updated_at` range; or return a distinct sentinel error for "device not found" vs "limit reached". — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/anonstore/database.go

pairs.golden: Time window calculation inconsistency: Using device.UpdatedAt.UTC().Add(-anonymousDeviceExpiration) as the lower bound but device.UpdatedAt as the current time may not match the intended logic. Consider using time.Now().UTC() consistently.
pairs.candidate: ErrDeviceLimitReached returned from Authenticate blocks anonymous users from accessing Grafana instead of just stopping device tracking — WHAT: When the device limit is reached, `ErrDeviceLimitReached` is propagated from `TagDevice` → `Authenticate`, causing `Authenticate` to return an error that rejects the anonymous user entirely. WHY: Anonymous authentication should still succeed when the device limit is reached — the limit should only stop tracking new devices, not block access. This means any instance with `device_limit` configured will deny anonymous access to new devices rather than just stopping to track them. HOW: Do not return the error from `Authenticate`; instead just log it (similar to other non-fatal tagging errors). — bug — pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/client.go

pairs.golden: SSRF vulnerability using open(url) without validation
pairs.candidate: Unguarded `i.content` call crashes the poll job on any RSS item that omits the content field — WHAT: `i.content` is accessed without a nil guard on every RSS feed item inside `poll_feed`. WHY: Many valid RSS/Atom feeds omit the `content` element (using `summary`, `description`, or `content_encoded` instead), causing `nil.scrub` to raise a `NoMethodError` that aborts the entire polling job and silently prevents all items from being imported. HOW: Guard with a safe-navigation operator or fallback: `content_raw = i.content || i.summary || i[:content_encoded] || ''; content = CGI.unescapeHTML(content_raw.scrub)`. — bug — app/jobs/scheduled/poll_feed.rb

pairs.golden: SSRF vulnerability using open(url) without validation
pairs.candidate: `revise!` does not exist on PostRevisor — every embed content update raises NoMethodError — WHAT: `PostRevisor.new(post).revise!(...)` is called with a bang (`!`) method that does not exist — only `revise` (without bang) is defined in the codebase. WHY: Every time an already-imported embed's content changes, the update path raises `NoMethodError: undefined method 'revise!' for #<PostRevisor>`, silently breaking all content refreshes for previously-imported posts. HOW: Replace `revisor.revise!` with `revisor.revise`. — bug — app/models/topic_embed.rb

pairs.golden: SSRF vulnerability using open(url) without validation
pairs.candidate: Full RSS feed is re-fetched and re-processed synchronously per concurrent retrieve_topic job, causing unbounded N×M fanout — WHAT: `perform_retrieve` calls `Jobs::PollFeed.new.execute({})` synchronously and inline every time a user visits an embed URL that has no associated topic. WHY: This causes a full RSS feed HTTP fetch and database-write loop for every concurrent `retrieve_topic` job, meaning N simultaneous users viewing new articles each independently trigger a full feed re-poll — an O(N-users × N-items) fanout of blocking network+DB work with no deduplication between concurrent job executions. HOW: Instead of calling `PollFeed` inline, enqueue it as an async job or use a distributed lock (e.g., a Redis key) so only one feed poll runs at a time across all concurrent retrieve_topic jobs. — bug — lib/topic_retriever.rb

pairs.golden: SSRF vulnerability using open(url) without validation
pairs.candidate: Unguarded `Kernel#open(url)` in import_remote enables SSRF against internal network addresses — WHAT: `open(url)` in `import_remote` uses Ruby's `Kernel#open`, which — when `open-uri` is loaded — can be pointed at any reachable internal network address because the only host validation happens earlier in `TopicRetriever#invalid_host?` on the original `embed_url`, but `import_remote` can also be called directly (e.g. from `fetch_http`) with an attacker-influenced URL that passed only a simple host equality check. WHY: An admin who sets `embeddable_host` to an internal hostname, or any path where `import_remote` is reached with a partially-validated URL, enables SSRF to internal services. HOW: Replace `open(url)` with an explicit `Net::HTTP` or `Faraday` call restricted to HTTP/HTTPS schemes, and validate the resolved IP is not in a private range before fetching. — bug — app/models/topic_embed.rb

pairs.golden: SSRF vulnerability using open(url) without validation
pairs.candidate: Nil dereference crash when RSS item has no `<content>` element — WHAT: `i.content` is accessed without a nil guard on line 35 — many valid RSS 2.0 feed items have no `<content>` element (they use only `<description>`), so `i.content` returns nil, and calling `.scrub` on nil raises `NoMethodError`. WHY: A single item without a content element crashes the entire `poll_feed` loop, silently leaving all subsequent feed items unprocessed and preventing any new topics from being imported for that polling cycle. HOW: Fall back to `i.description` (the RSS 2.0 body field) when content is absent: `content = CGI.unescapeHTML((i.content || i.description || '').to_s.scrub)`. — bug — app/jobs/scheduled/poll_feed.rb

pairs.golden: Shared mutable default in dataclass timestamp
pairs.candidate: `timezone.now()` is evaluated once at import time as a class-level default, so all AssignmentSource instances share the same stale timestamp instead of capturing the time of construction. — WHAT: The `queued` field default `timezone.now()` is evaluated once at class/module import time, not at instance creation time, because Python dataclass field defaults are not callables — they are evaluated eagerly. WHY: Every `AssignmentSource` instance that omits `queued` (i.e., every instance created via `from_integration`) will carry the exact same stale timestamp (the moment the module was first imported), making the `queued` field meaningless as a timing signal. HOW: Use `dataclasses.field(default_factory=timezone.now)` so that `timezone.now()` is called freshly for each new instance. — bug — src/sentry/integrations/services/assignment_source.py

pairs.candidate: Copy-paste error: `rawTokenId` null check incorrectly validates `grantType` again, leaving `rawTokenId` unguarded and allowing null to silently corrupt encoded token IDs. — WHAT: `Objects.requireNonNull` on line 73 validates `grantType` a second time instead of validating `rawTokenId`, so a null `rawTokenId` passes the constructor guard silently. HOW: Replace `credentialKey.access_token` with `accessTokenParsed.data.access_token` and `credentialKey.instance_url` with `accessTokenParsed.data.instance_url` when constructing the `jsforce.Connection`. — bug — packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts

pairs.golden: The parseRefreshTokenResponse function incorrectly sets refresh_token to the hardcoded string 'refresh_token' when it's missing from the OAuth refresh token response. This invalidates the token, breaking subsequent token refreshes and causing authentication failures.
pairs.candidate: Unhandled ZodError on malformed webhook body causes a 500 instead of a clean 400 — WHAT: `appCredentialWebhookRequestBodySchema.parse(req.body)` throws an unhandled `ZodError` when the request body is malformed or missing required fields, with no surrounding try/catch. WHY: The unhandled exception produces an unformatted 500 response instead of a descriptive 400, leaking a stack trace and breaking the caller's ability to diagnose the error. HOW: Use `safeParse` and return a 400 on failure, or wrap in try/catch. — bug — apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts

pairs.golden: The parseRefreshTokenResponse function incorrectly sets refresh_token to the hardcoded string 'refresh_token' when it's missing from the OAuth refresh token response. This invalidates the token, breaking subsequent token refreshes and causing authentication failures.
pairs.candidate: Missing encryption key env var causes symmetricDecrypt to be called with an empty string, crashing the handler — WHAT: `symmetricDecrypt` is called with an empty string as the encryption key when `CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY` is not set (`process.env.CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY || ""`). WHY: AES-256 requires a 32-byte key; an empty string will cause the decrypt call to throw or produce garbage, crashing the handler for every request when the env var is missing. HOW: Validate that `CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY` is set before attempting decryption, returning a 500 with a clear configuration error. — bug — apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts

pairs.golden: Invalid Zod schema syntax. Computed property keys like [z.string().toString()] are not valid in Zod object schemas and will cause runtime errors.
pairs.candidate: Salesforce jsforce.Connection is initialized with the stale pre-refresh access token instead of the newly obtained one — WHAT: The newly fetched access token from the refresh response is never used to initialize the `jsforce.Connection`; the connection is built with `credentialKey.access_token` and `credentialKey.instance_url`, which are the stale pre-refresh values from the stored credential. WHY: Every Salesforce connection starts with the old (possibly expired) access token, so any call on a truly expired credential will fail immediately despite the refresh having succeeded and persisted a valid new token. HOW: Replace `credentialKey.access_token` with `accessTokenParsed.data.access_token` and `credentialKey.instance_url` with `accessTokenParsed.data.instance_url` when constructing the `jsforce.Connection`. — bug — packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts

pairs.golden: Invalid Zod schema syntax. Computed property keys like [z.string().toString()] are not valid in Zod object schemas and will cause runtime errors.
pairs.candidate: Webhook auth check passes when CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET env var is unset because both sides of the comparison are undefined — WHAT: When `CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` is not set in the environment, `process.env.CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` resolves to `undefined`, and the check `req.headers[...] !== undefined` is `false` whenever the request also omits the header (header value also `undefined`), so the guard passes and the endpoint is fully unauthenticated. WHY: Any caller that simply omits the webhook secret header can write arbitrary credentials into any user's account when the env var is not configured. HOW: Explicitly reject the request if `CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` is not set, and use a constant-time comparison to avoid timing attacks. — bug — apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts

pairs.golden: Invalid Zod schema syntax. Computed property keys like [z.string().toString()] are not valid in Zod object schemas and will cause runtime errors.
pairs.candidate: Zod parse() throws an unhandled exception on malformed request body, producing an unstructured 500 instead of a 400 — WHAT: `appCredentialWebhookRequestBodySchema.parse(req.body)` throws an unhandled `ZodError` when the request body is malformed (e.g., `userId` is a string, `keys` is absent). WHY: Next.js does not catch ZodError and returns a 500 with a stack trace instead of a structured 400, potentially leaking internal schema details to callers. HOW: Use `safeParse` and return a 400 on failure. — bug — apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts

pairs.golden: Invalid Zod schema syntax. Computed property keys like [z.string().toString()] are not valid in Zod object schemas and will cause runtime errors.
pairs.candidate: Missing CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY falls back to empty string, causing a runtime crash or no-op encryption — WHAT: `symmetricDecrypt` is called with an empty string as the key (`process.env.CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY || ""`) when the env var is not configured. WHY: AES-256 requires a 32-byte key; passing an empty string will throw a runtime exception (crashing the request with a 500) or, if the crypto utility accepts it, silently decrypt with a zero key, rendering the encryption meaningless. HOW: Explicitly check that `CALCOM_APP_CREDENTIAL_ENCRYPTION_KEY` is set and return a 500/configuration-error response if it is not. — bug — apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts

pairs.golden: Invalid Zod schema syntax. Computed property keys like [z.string().toString()] are not valid in Zod object schemas and will cause runtime errors.
pairs.candidate: Salesforce Connection is initialized with the stale pre-refresh access token instead of the newly fetched one — WHAT: The newly fetched access token (`accessTokenParsed.data.access_token`) is saved to the database but the stale token from the original `credentialKey` (read before the refresh) is what gets passed to `new jsforce.Connection({ accessToken: credentialKey.access_token })`. WHY: Every API call made through this connection will use the expired access token, causing Salesforce to reject requests with 401 errors after the old token expires. HOW: Use `accessTokenParsed.data.access_token` and `accessTokenParsed.data.instance_url` when constructing the Connection. — bug — packages/app-store/salesforce/lib/CalendarService.ts

pairs.golden: Invalid Zod schema syntax. Computed property keys like [z.string().toString()] are not valid in Zod object schemas and will cause runtime errors.
pairs.candidate: Missing CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET env var causes the auth check to pass for any request that omits the header — WHAT: When `CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` is not configured, `process.env.CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` is `undefined`, and a request sent without the webhook header also produces `undefined` for `req.headers[...]`; the check `undefined !== undefined` evaluates to `false`, so the guard passes and the unauthenticated request is accepted. WHY: Any caller who omits the secret header entirely bypasses authentication when the server has no secret configured, allowing arbitrary credential writes. HOW: Explicitly reject the request when the secret env var is not set, and treat a missing header as a failed check. — bug — apps/web/pages/api/webhook/app-credential.ts
